CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from motivation through methods (annotation and representation projection), experiments, and conclusions; the two main contributions are stated early and remain the focus. Notation appears consistent and the description of the q(y) selection metric and thresholds q and n is sufficiently precise for understanding, although more detail on threshold selection could aid reproducibility. Figures/tables (results on CoNLL and in-house datasets) and examples are reported as informative and the writing quality is generally good with few ambiguities. From a reproducibility-oriented reader’s perspective the presentation is strong, with only minor clarifications needed around data-selection tuning and release of code/data to fully support replication. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation through methods (annotation and representation projection), experiments, and conclusions, and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and the paper reports hyperparameters and datasets clearly; figures/tables and the proposed q(y) selection metric are described in ways that aid reproducibility. The writing quality is generally good, though the heuristic data-selection tuning (q and n) and some practical choices (e.g., omission of gazetteers/char-level features) could use slightly more explicit guidance for readers applying the method to new languages.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from motivation through methods (annotation and representation projection), experiments, and conclusions; the two main contributions are stated early and remain the focus. Notation appears consistent and the description of the q(y) selection metric and thresholds q and n is sufficiently precise for understanding, although more detail on threshold selection could aid reproducibility. Figures/tables (results on CoNLL and in-house datasets) and examples are reported as informative and the writing quality is generally good with few ambiguities. From a reproducibility-oriented reader’s perspective the presentation is strong, with only minor clarifications needed around data-selection tuning and release of code/data to fully support replication.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation through methods (annotation and representation projection), experiments, and conclusions, and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and the paper reports hyperparameters and datasets clearly; figures/tables and the proposed q(y) selection metric are described in ways that aid reproducibility. The writing quality is generally good, though the heuristic data-selection tuning (q and n) and some practical choices (e.g., omission of gazetteers/char-level features) could use slightly more explicit guidance for readers applying the method to new languages.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses cross-lingual NER with representation and annotation projection methods and neural architectures, which squarely fits top-tier NLP/ML conference topics (transfer learning, low-resource NLP). Evaluation uses standard CoNLL benchmarks and comparisons to relevant prior work (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016), and reports appropriate metrics and hyperparameters. My only reservation is reproducibility: several in-house annotated datasets and no mention of released code/data limit immediate community adoption, so while the contribution is appropriate for the venue, minor scope concerns about reproducibility remain. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venues: it tackles cross-lingual NER using projection and embedding-mapping techniques that are of clear interest to the community and evaluates on standard CoNLL benchmarks plus additional in-house languages. The problem formulation, the two complementary projection strategies, the co-decoding schemes, and F1-based evaluation match the methodological expectations for the venue. My only reservation is that the contribution is an empirical combination and heuristic selection scheme rather than a major new theoretical advance, so it is a strong practical paper that may be seen as incremental by reviewers seeking cutting-edge modeling novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses cross-lingual NER with representation and annotation projection methods and neural architectures, which squarely fits top-tier NLP/ML conference topics (transfer learning, low-resource NLP). Evaluation uses standard CoNLL benchmarks and comparisons to relevant prior work (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016), and reports appropriate metrics and hyperparameters. My only reservation is reproducibility: several in-house annotated datasets and no mention of released code/data limit immediate community adoption, so while the contribution is appropriate for the venue, minor scope concerns about reproducibility remain.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venues: it tackles cross-lingual NER using projection and embedding-mapping techniques that are of clear interest to the community and evaluates on standard CoNLL benchmarks plus additional in-house languages. The problem formulation, the two complementary projection strategies, the co-decoding schemes, and F1-based evaluation match the methodological expectations for the venue. My only reservation is that the contribution is an empirical combination and heuristic selection scheme rather than a major new theoretical advance, so it is a strong practical paper that may be seen as incremental by reviewers seeking cutting-edge modeling novelty.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its two main contributions (annotation projection with a frequency-based sentence selection and representation projection via linear embedding mapping) and presents experimental results showing improvements over the two prior baselines on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, supporting the core claims. However, reproducibility and methodological rigor have minor gaps: the selection heuristic requires tuning of thresholds (q, n) with no reported sensitivity analyses or statistical significance testing, and code/data release is not mentioned. Mathematical parts appear straightforward (linear mapping) so no substantive derivation issues are evident from the summary. Overall the results seem credible but would benefit from ablation/sensitivity studies and clearer reproducibility artifacts to fully validate claims. | B(4): Overall the paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons showing that combining annotation and representation projection with co-decoding outperforms the cited baselines on CoNLL data and approaches supervised performance for some languages. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check; the technical methods (linear embedding mapping, projection-based labeling, and neural decoders NN1/NN2) are described and evaluated, but the experimental methodology has some limitations: key details such as statistical significance testing are not reported and some important evaluations rely on in-house datasets (Japanese/Korean/German/Portuguese) which makes reproducibility and assessment of generality harder. There are also minor but impacting assumptions (e.g., effectiveness of linear mapping across very distant languages, and tuning of selection thresholds q and n) that are acknowledged but not deeply analyzed; nonetheless the presented results reasonably justify the paper's conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its two main contributions (annotation projection with a frequency-based sentence selection and representation projection via linear embedding mapping) and presents experimental results showing improvements over the two prior baselines on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, supporting the core claims. However, reproducibility and methodological rigor have minor gaps: the selection heuristic requires tuning of thresholds (q, n) with no reported sensitivity analyses or statistical significance testing, and code/data release is not mentioned. Mathematical parts appear straightforward (linear mapping) so no substantive derivation issues are evident from the summary. Overall the results seem credible but would benefit from ablation/sensitivity studies and clearer reproducibility artifacts to fully validate claims.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons showing that combining annotation and representation projection with co-decoding outperforms the cited baselines on CoNLL data and approaches supervised performance for some languages. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check; the technical methods (linear embedding mapping, projection-based labeling, and neural decoders NN1/NN2) are described and evaluated, but the experimental methodology has some limitations: key details such as statistical significance testing are not reported and some important evaluations rely on in-house datasets (Japanese/Korean/German/Portuguese) which makes reproducibility and assessment of generality harder. There are also minor but impacting assumptions (e.g., effectiveness of linear mapping across very distant languages, and tuning of selection thresholds q and n) that are acknowledged but not deeply analyzed; nonetheless the presented results reasonably justify the paper's conclusions.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—and proposes two complementary, scalable approaches (annotation projection with a principled sentence selection metric q(y) and representation projection via linear embedding maps) plus novel co-decoding schemes; experiments across CoNLL languages and several in-house languages show improvements over prior work and near-supervised performance for some languages. From my empirical perspective, the combination of a data-selection heuristic (q and n), simple English-only neural models (NN1/NN2), and language-agnostic representation projection is likely to influence how practitioners build low-resource NER systems because it balances practicality and performance without language-specific resources. It is not fully transformative because it relies on heuristics (q,n) and uses standard linear mapping techniques, but it opens clear, useful directions for follow-up work on selection robustness, co-decoding strategies, and broader multilingual deployment. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—scalable weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—by combining complementary projection paradigms (annotation projection with a principled sentence selection heuristic and representation projection via embedding mapping) and introducing co-decoding schemes that materially improve results over prior projection baselines on CoNLL and several in-house languages. The approach is practically attractive because a single English NER model can be applied to many target languages without retraining, and the selection metric q(y) addresses a real-world tradeoff between label quality and data quantity. While the need to tune selection thresholds and omission of character/gazetteer features limit out-of-the-box applicability in very low-resource settings, the work is likely to influence how researchers combine projection signals and to be built upon for broader multilingual transfer methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—and proposes two complementary, scalable approaches (annotation projection with a principled sentence selection metric q(y) and representation projection via linear embedding maps) plus novel co-decoding schemes; experiments across CoNLL languages and several in-house languages show improvements over prior work and near-supervised performance for some languages. From my empirical perspective, the combination of a data-selection heuristic (q and n), simple English-only neural models (NN1/NN2), and language-agnostic representation projection is likely to influence how practitioners build low-resource NER systems because it balances practicality and performance without language-specific resources. It is not fully transformative because it relies on heuristics (q,n) and uses standard linear mapping techniques, but it opens clear, useful directions for follow-up work on selection robustness, co-decoding strategies, and broader multilingual deployment.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—scalable weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—by combining complementary projection paradigms (annotation projection with a principled sentence selection heuristic and representation projection via embedding mapping) and introducing co-decoding schemes that materially improve results over prior projection baselines on CoNLL and several in-house languages. The approach is practically attractive because a single English NER model can be applied to many target languages without retraining, and the selection metric q(y) addresses a real-world tradeoff between label quality and data quantity. While the need to tune selection thresholds and omission of character/gazetteer features limit out-of-the-box applicability in very low-resource settings, the work is likely to influence how researchers combine projection signals and to be built upon for broader multilingual transfer methods.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper combines known directions (annotation projection and embedding-space projection) into a practical pipeline and adds useful engineering novelties (a frequency-based data-selection score q(y) with thresholds, co-decoding heuristics, and the NN2 prototype smoothing layer). These elements are meaningful refinements rather than fundamentally new paradigms: embedding mapping to apply an English model has antecedents in cross-lingual embedding work, and annotation projection has long been used for weak supervision. The contribution is a solid, moderately novel integration and some non-trivial extensions (NN2, co-decoding, q/n selection) that would inform practitioners, but it is best characterized as incremental rather than highly original. | B(4): The paper brings together two complementary projection strategies (annotation projection with a novel frequency-based sentence selection metric q(y) and representation projection via linear embedding mapping so a single English NER can be reused) and proposes concrete co-decoding schemes to fuse them—an approach that is a meaningful and practical advance over prior projection-only works (e.g. Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016). The introduction of the NN2 smoothing layer (prototype vectors) and the explicit quality/coverage tradeoff for selecting projected sentences are non-trivial technical additions rather than off-the-shelf combinations. That said, the paper builds on a well-studied cross-lingual projection and embedding-mapping literature, so its contribution is notable and well-differentiated but not a radical departure from existing paradigms.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines known directions (annotation projection and embedding-space projection) into a practical pipeline and adds useful engineering novelties (a frequency-based data-selection score q(y) with thresholds, co-decoding heuristics, and the NN2 prototype smoothing layer). These elements are meaningful refinements rather than fundamentally new paradigms: embedding mapping to apply an English model has antecedents in cross-lingual embedding work, and annotation projection has long been used for weak supervision. The contribution is a solid, moderately novel integration and some non-trivial extensions (NN2, co-decoding, q/n selection) that would inform practitioners, but it is best characterized as incremental rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper brings together two complementary projection strategies (annotation projection with a novel frequency-based sentence selection metric q(y) and representation projection via linear embedding mapping so a single English NER can be reused) and proposes concrete co-decoding schemes to fuse them—an approach that is a meaningful and practical advance over prior projection-only works (e.g. Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016). The introduction of the NN2 smoothing layer (prototype vectors) and the explicit quality/coverage tradeoff for selecting projected sentences are non-trivial technical additions rather than off-the-shelf combinations. That said, the paper builds on a well-studied cross-lingual projection and embedding-mapping literature, so its contribution is notable and well-differentiated but not a radical departure from existing paradigms.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against two established prior methods (Täckström et al. 2012; Tsai et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets with F1/precision/recall, which supports largely fair, reproducible comparisons. However, it omits some recent and directly relevant alternatives (e.g., cheap-translation/lexicon-based strategies and wikification-based transfer methods from ~2016–2017) that address the same low-resource cross-lingual NER objective. It is also not fully clear from the summary whether all baselines were run under identical training/evaluation conditions for the in-house language datasets, and the paper does not explicitly report when prior methods perform comparably or worse beyond claiming overall improvements. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions and limited discussion of parity with other recent baselines. | B(4): The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets and compares against two established projection baselines (Täc kström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) using the same metrics, which makes the comparisons largely fair. The authors analyze differences between annotation- and representation-projection components, introduce sensible co-decoding schemes, and acknowledge limitations of their selection heuristic. However, some recent and conceptually relevant alternatives (e.g., translation/lexicon-based approaches such as ‘Cheap Translation for Cross-Lingual NER’ and wikification-based transfer methods) are not included as empirical baselines, so the comparison is not fully comprehensive for the current cross-lingual NER landscape.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against two established prior methods (Täckström et al. 2012; Tsai et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets with F1/precision/recall, which supports largely fair, reproducible comparisons. However, it omits some recent and directly relevant alternatives (e.g., cheap-translation/lexicon-based strategies and wikification-based transfer methods from ~2016–2017) that address the same low-resource cross-lingual NER objective. It is also not fully clear from the summary whether all baselines were run under identical training/evaluation conditions for the in-house language datasets, and the paper does not explicitly report when prior methods perform comparably or worse beyond claiming overall improvements. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions and limited discussion of parity with other recent baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets and compares against two established projection baselines (Täc kström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) using the same metrics, which makes the comparisons largely fair. The authors analyze differences between annotation- and representation-projection components, introduce sensible co-decoding schemes, and acknowledge limitations of their selection heuristic. However, some recent and conceptually relevant alternatives (e.g., translation/lexicon-based approaches such as ‘Cheap Translation for Cross-Lingual NER’ and wikification-based transfer methods) are not included as empirical baselines, so the comparison is not fully comprehensive for the current cross-lingual NER landscape.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents solid experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) across CoNLL languages and several in-house datasets, and evaluates both annotation- and representation-projection approaches plus co-decoding and two neural architectures (NN1/NN2). It includes a sensible data-selection metric (q,n), reports hyperparameters, and discusses the main limitation (threshold tuning and omission of gazetteers/char features). However, reproducibility is incomplete (no code release mentioned and some implementation/alignment/mapping details needed to fully reproduce), and sensitivity analyses/ablation over the selection thresholds and cross-lingual mapping choices appear limited—so the empirical work is strong but missing a few robustness and release elements I would expect. | B(4): The experimental suite is fairly comprehensive: multiple languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese/German), comparisons to two prior projection baselines, and evaluations of the two projection methods plus co-decoding. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is partial—there is tuning and discussion of the annotation-selection thresholds q and n and some model variants (NN1 vs NN2 and two co-decoders) but no systematic ablation isolating each design choice (e.g., impact of prototype smoothing vs. more architectures) or robustness across truly low-resource parallel sizes. Related work is reported as adequate, and limitations (threshold tuning, omission of gazetteers/char features) are acknowledged, but reproducibility is hindered by use of in-house test sets and no public code/data release despite reported hyperparameters. These strengths and gaps justify a solid but not perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents solid experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) across CoNLL languages and several in-house datasets, and evaluates both annotation- and representation-projection approaches plus co-decoding and two neural architectures (NN1/NN2). It includes a sensible data-selection metric (q,n), reports hyperparameters, and discusses the main limitation (threshold tuning and omission of gazetteers/char features). However, reproducibility is incomplete (no code release mentioned and some implementation/alignment/mapping details needed to fully reproduce), and sensitivity analyses/ablation over the selection thresholds and cross-lingual mapping choices appear limited—so the empirical work is strong but missing a few robustness and release elements I would expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental suite is fairly comprehensive: multiple languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese/German), comparisons to two prior projection baselines, and evaluations of the two projection methods plus co-decoding. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is partial—there is tuning and discussion of the annotation-selection thresholds q and n and some model variants (NN1 vs NN2 and two co-decoders) but no systematic ablation isolating each design choice (e.g., impact of prototype smoothing vs. more architectures) or robustness across truly low-resource parallel sizes. Related work is reported as adequate, and limitations (threshold tuning, omission of gazetteers/char features) are acknowledged, but reproducibility is hindered by use of in-house test sets and no public code/data release despite reported hyperparameters. These strengths and gaps justify a solid but not perfect score.

main_claims: Proposes two weakly supervised cross-lingual NER approaches: (1) annotation projection with a heuristic data selection scheme to extract good-quality weakly labeled target-language data, and (2) representation projection that maps target-language word embeddings into English so a single English NER model can be applied to many languages without retraining.

Introduces two neural NER architectures (NN1 using embeddings, NN2 adding a smoothing layer of prototype vectors) and two co-decoding schemes (exclude-O confidence-based and rank-based) to combine the projection-based methods.

Shows experimentally that combining annotation projection and representation projection via co-decoding outperforms two prior cross-lingual NER approaches (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) on CoNLL test data and approaches supervised performance for some languages.

Presents a data selection metric q(y) based on projected-entity frequency tables and a thresholding scheme (q and n) to trade off annotation quality, information amount, and number of selected sentences.
_summary.methodology: Train an English NER system that uses only word embeddings (NN1 or NN2); (1) produce weak target-language labels by annotation projection from English via alignments and select high-quality projected sentences using a frequency-based quality score q(y); (2) train monolingual embeddings and learn linear mappings to project target embeddings into English space and decode with the English NER model; combine outputs with two co-decoding schemes.
datasets: In-house human-annotated NER data for Japanese
In-house human-annotated NER data for Korean
In-house human-annotated NER data for German (fine-grained, >50 entity types)
In-house human-annotated NER data for Portuguese (fine-grained, >50 entity types)
limitations: The heuristic data selection requires tuning two thresholds q (quality) and n (minimum entity count) to balance annotation quality, information amount, and number of sentences selected.
The neural models intentionally do not use gazetteers or character-level representations because such features may not be readily available or transferable to other languages (design choice noted).

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Empiricist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience in building and evaluating NLP systems, particularly transfer-learning and low-resource pipelines; has contributed to reproducible benchmark suites and open-source toolkits for NER and embedding alignment.
traits: carefully inspects experimental design, hyperparameter selection, and training/validation/test splits for leakage
expects strong baselines, ablation studies, and significance testing to support claims about improvements
values clear implementation details (alignment tools, embedding training recipes, mapping algorithms) and encourages release of code and data selection scripts
looks for robustness checks across languages and sensitivity analyses to thresholds (q, n) and co-decoding choices
notes runtime, model size, and practical deployment considerations (e.g., need for parallel data or monolingual corpora)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on empirical rigor and reproducibility: checks whether experiments isolate the contribution, whether comparisons are fair, and whether the methods are described in enough detail to reproduce results.

reviewer_b.name: Cross-Lingual Systems Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching multilingual NLP and cross-lingual transfer, with a track record of work on projection methods, representation learning, and real-world low-resource language applications.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior projection and embedding-mapping methods and how the ideas advance the field
assesses practical impact: which languages and resource regimes benefit, and whether the approach scales to truly low-resource settings
checks conceptual clarity of combining annotation and representation projection and whether the proposed co-decoding schemes offer meaningful conceptual contributions
interested in limitations, failure cases, and broader implications for multilingual evaluation and future work direction
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes positioning and significance: judges the paper by its novelty, relevance to ongoing challenges in cross-lingual NLP, and the clarity of its conceptual contributions and limitations.
